description: Curated nutrient form quality tiers. Tier 1 = high bioavailability. Tier 4 = synthetic/problematic.

forms.rationale: Chelated form, superior absorption and GI tolerability.
Bis-glycinate chelate, equivalent to glycinate in bioavailability.
Malic acid chelate, well-tolerated, good absorption.
Soluble salt, moderate bioavailability, laxative at high doses.
Crosses blood-brain barrier, cognitive applications.
Low bioavailability (~4%), cheap filler form.
Superior absorption vs inorganic forms.
Chelated form, good bioavailability and tolerability.
Bis-glycinate chelate, superior to oxide or sulfate.
Good water solubility, moderate absorption.
Moderate bioavailability, commonly used.
Low bioavailability, cheap filler form.
Inorganic form, moderate bioavailability but significant GI side effects.
Chelated form, superior absorption and fewer GI side effects.
Moderate bioavailability, gentler than sulfate.
Moderate bioavailability, commonly used.
High GI side effect profile, oxidative stress potential.
Absorbed without food, suitable for low-acid conditions.
Requires stomach acid for absorption; cheapest form.
Moderate bioavailability, co-supplement with phosphorus.
Active coenzyme form, no conversion required.
Pyridoxal-5-phosphate — active form.
Commonly used precursor, requires conversion to active form.
Precursor form, requires hepatic conversion.
Active methylfolate — bypasses MTHFR polymorphism.
Active form, directly usable without conversion.
Synthetic form requiring conversion; reduced efficacy in MTHFR variants.
Active methyl form, superior retention.
Active mitochondrial form.
Natural form, good retention.
Synthetic form, requires conversion, low-cost.
Organic form, superior bioavailability vs inorganic.
Inorganic form, lower bioavailability and higher toxicity risk.
D3 form, superior at raising serum 25(OH)D.
D2 form, less potent than D3 at equivalent doses.
Natural form, highest bioavailability.
Synthetic racemic mixture, lower bioavailability.
